speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is February 2, 1997. The survivor is Sidney Glucksman. The interviewer is Jaclyn Jeffrey, the city, New Haven, the country, the United States. And this interview will be conducted in English. Today's date is February 2, 1997. The survivor is Sidney Glucksman, the interviewer Jaclyn Jeffrey, the city, New Haven, the country, the United States. And this interview we conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mr. Glucksman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's start first if you could spell for me your full first and last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sidney, S-I-D-N-E-Y, Glucksman, G-L-U-C-K-S-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birthplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland-- Chrznow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-R-Z-N-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that the city in which you grew up as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Describe, if you would, what it looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a nice Jewish community. We had a lot of Jewish yeshivas.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a city was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What about your home? What did your house or your apartment look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a nice, little house. And we had a little factory set up in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailors. And we all worked there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in the family? There was your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had another brother, sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina-- Rachel, in Jewish. Regina.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name was Cela. And my brother's name was-- well, now it would be Solomon. That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had one brother and one sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the brother was still a little kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your sister? Was she older or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was family life like that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made it special?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had the whole family. We had the grandmother. We had the grandfather. I even had a great grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they all live in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but not far. Lived in walking distance. It was very close by.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the holidays like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about one you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: One I remember-- it was like the purpose of the whole family. You know, we all belonged to the same synagogue. So they all took the same-- the children to the synagogue. After the synagogue, we all went to celebrate to grandfather's house. And it was a big family. My grandmother must have had like, three sons and two daughters. And they all had children. So it was a beautiful gathering. And my grandfather would-- you know, make up the Seder. And it was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your father do? He was a tailor you said before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And that work-- and the work was all done out of the house? Or did he have a shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: All out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was set up like a shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the inside of the house look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, nice, a normal house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in a big part of the city? Was it very populated?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very populated, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have to share a room with your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did share with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my little  brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He would crawl into my bed every morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would play . It was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you close with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the shop was in the house, or the factory was in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said before you all helped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your job? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to-- I was seven years old when I-- they started me off near a machine, just to make pockets to pants. That was the easiest job. You know, it was not a motorized machine. It was a machine that you use your legs, your feet--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pedal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would just sew around the pockets-- you know, just little seams, then turn them inside out and pile them up to the ceiling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that right? And what were your brother and sister's jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had their own-- each one has-- had his own job. My brother would be the one-- the older brother-- I mean, my older sister. She did connect the legs from the pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you all work together at the same time, the whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Homemaking. She would cook. She would prepare everything for the children-- for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it a close family, the immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. Very close. I have a picture of my mother. That's the only thing that was already-- it was-- I didn't have that picture. But somebody from Israel gave it to me, which it was sort of ruined and I brought it back. And I  have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you have it today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it today, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: From my father, I don't have any pictures. From my bother, I don't-- I don't have anything. From my sister, I don't have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about school, your friends, what you liked about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice. We all went-- all walked to school. Especially it was nice, like in wintertime, there was no lighting in the town. And we had to work at night late, like seven o'clock, when it became dark with lanterns in the hands, with the candle lit up inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you walk with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the rest of the kids who lived around my neighborhood, my street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the names of any of the other kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the pictures of them. One was Slamek Gross. You know, Maniek-- I keep forgetting the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I've-- Maniek Siegel. Joe Schnitzer, who was my-- the best man on my wedding, passed away. He is in the picture over there. But you look at it, you know, he looks like a black guy. You saw it?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not yet. Uh-uh.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, if you look at the picture, you would think  he is colored.

words.text: if you look at the picture,

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that right? [LAUGHS] OK. And so you would walk with lanterns in dark streets to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What did you like best about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was nice. We made fun out of the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was from another town where they spoke a different Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, like I am from [? Galicia. ?] And he was from Lithuania. And that-- like they would call potatoes. You know, like he would call it [YIDDISH]. So we made fun out of him, because we never heard of anybody calling a potato a [YIDDISH]. And the he slept at the cheder. They said that he slept on a bench, because he had quite a long way to travel home for weekends. So he would stay over weekends in the cheder and sleep on a bench.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe. They called it-- they called it Moishe [YIDDISH], which it meant he had a little beard like a goat. So that's we called him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your favorite hobbies or activities when you were a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, playing soccer. That was my main thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about for your sister and brother? Did you play together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we played together when-- like children, you know, in the backyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of games did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, usually we had played like we would make a little hole in the ground with our heels from the shoes. That was in summertime. And we would play with nuts-- six nuts. You had to try to throw it in the little hole, either two or four. If they came out you won. If three or five or one came out, you lost. So you'd take the six nuts. Then three-- then you have to start off again. There were three nuts against three nuts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you often win when you played?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a lot of times, because I came home with pockets full of nuts. So I know I won.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in the town among Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. When a high holiday came around or in-- on the Shabbos, most of the stores-- they would be closed. Because most of the Jewish stores-- most were Jewish stores. So the gentiles-- they knew that they have to prepare the food, the bread enough to last them over like, Passover. So they had to buy for seven days. Otherwise, the ones who baked, they could bake their own. Otherwise, the stores would be closed to buy flour even.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father have non-Jewish customers with his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, he was a contractor. Most of the stuff that he made-- I  remember sometimes-- so I delivered it on a little wagon to the stores. So most of it was like, contracting to the stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about friends? Did you have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that  I went to school together, or they lived around my neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were those relationships like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you notice-- first notice things start to change between Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't until the last minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: That happ-- that happened one day in 1939. We came home from school late in the-- late in the afternoon. People started to talk that we're not going to be able to sleep home that night, that we are going to have to hide in shelters, because they heard that the Germans are very close by. Across the street from us lived one of my uncles. And in that house, he had a big basement. When dark came around, my parents took the whole family across the street to sleep in the basement there, because we didn't have that basement. And during the night, we heard louder noises-- guns, you know, like from tanks-- shootings-- bombarding. And everybody curled up. And it went on like this until daylight. Daylight came around. Everybody was anxious to see what's going on. They opened up a little doors to look out. And what we did see was turned over tanks in the streets, a lot of dead soldiers laying in the streets. And we saw already a lot of other uniforms-- men in dark gray uniforms walking around all over. So we knew that they're Germans, because nobody else was expected to fight at that time. So that was the only thing that I remember seeing when I got up the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in 1939, you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, prior to that moment and that day when you went to your uncle's house, had you seen any evidence of war that close?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was happening outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Uh-uh.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? You went out. You saw what was happening in the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back hiding. We were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back to your uncle's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were afraid to get out, that we're going to get shot. And there was nobody allowed in the street. It didn't matter Jewish or gentile-- nobody was allowed out. They were just looking to take over the city-- the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in the basement?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think a couple-- couple of nights, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was food prepared, yes. There was enough food there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so who was in the basement? Who specifically was there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would be your parents, your brother, your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my uncle, and aunt, and my grandmother, and grandfather. At that time, I don't remember if they were there. Or I think they stayed in their own place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your great grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was dead already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she had-- she had died?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of natural causes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was  94. She looked-- I can picture her now. She--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very short, you know, bent over. And she wore on her head like-- not a hat, but it was like something out of lace. And I still can picture her now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. While you were in the basement in your uncle's house, you said you were there for a couple of nights.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you come up from the basement during the day in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what do you recall doing during the course of the few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just being afraid. Just being afraid what's going to happen at the next minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Germans-- they just walked around all over to look into each house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall when a German came into your uncle's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one night you could hear the footsteps, because they-- when they were walking, even on the street, or on the sidewalk, or on a step, you could hear very loud noises, because they had like, nails in the soles of the boots or shoes. You know, a lot of those-- I don't know what you call this. But most the military at that time-- the Polish had it too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there lots of them? Were the streets infiltrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, they came in on horses, and motorcycles, and trucks, cars. Anything you want to think of, they came in on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they take over homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's as soon they took-- yes, they came in and they took over. I mean, they had to set-- they set up the shop right aways.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anyone set up shop in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or your uncle's home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had bigger homes to set up shop. That was not big enough for them. They took schools, gymnasium-- there had a lot of those big buildings to take over. And that's what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents try to talk with you and your brother and sister about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just talking about it to ask questions-- what's going to be now?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they respond to your questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, what it was just go on I think like nothing happened. But a few days later, they came out-- like outside, they had the plaques written on it, you know, that Jews are going to have to wear white arm bands with the Star of David. And every Jew should come to a certain place to collect the arm bands. And that's how it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you recall-- did you go that day to pick it up yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember if I went or my parents went. But I remember that they brought me home one and I wore it. And instead wearing it here, where we did supposed to, I wore it  lower.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I figured if I go someplace, that I don't want to be recognized. I could closely put it in, my hand into the pocket, and it would hide the band. You know, I could bring down a little lower and just put it into the pocket with my hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that band something you were required to wear all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in your own home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, your own home nobody saw you, so you didn't have to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: You took off the jacket, you didn't have to wear the band, because you were in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this point, were you still at your uncle's home? Or did you go back to your own home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was already in my own home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said just a moment ago that's how it started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: From that moment, what else began to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every day something different. They would close down most of the stores. The bakeries-- they left them open certain hours so they should be able to bake bread for the next day. And then people would come by. And there was a military-- you know, German soldiers right in the doorway there to watch, that told the baker how much to give to the person-- you know, half pound bread or a pound bread. I remember they sliced up breads in little pieces. You had to pay for it. But that's all you were allowed to get. And that baker in our street-- like, when I was outside, and I saw that there are no German staying near it, I would go in and ask him if he has any  more bread left that I could take. Once in a  while, he gave me even a whole bread. And I would put it under my jacket and try to walk home with it so we should have more to eat the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was daily life like as things started to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from the beginning, there was not much, except they took people out of the house to do some cleaning in the streets, cleaning in the places where there stayed the Germans. After the end of the day, they would allow you to go back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or anyone in your family have to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But later on, a few months later, one of the German police in the street would come over to me. He would grab my arm and take me. And I thought that he is going to arrest me. But meanwhile, he took me to a stable, where they had the horses. And they gave me a big brush to brush down the horses. But then they let me off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any school at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not go to school any after that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your father still able to do his tailoring?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did. He did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds, if any, of interactions were there between the Germans and people in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing that I can remember, really. I didn't follow everything through. I was, you know, 12 years old. I didn't pay any attention to that stuff much, unless what I saw with my own eyes, right in front of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did relationships between Jews and non-Jews change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our town was not too bad. We didn't have any pro-- progra-- no, what do they call it? You know, there were-- there were anti-Semites there, but they didn't show it. There  were no pogroms or anything like this, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still friendly, for example, with the boys that you walked to school with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father still have his non-Jewish customers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did this type of existence go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until they started taking the Jews and ship them out to the working camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- about how long after the initial occupation of your town did that occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say probably maybe about half to three-quarters of a year when they started it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: They--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- one days they went out and they put plaques on the streets that anybody up to a certain age should assemble in the school buildings or gymnasium buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly men. And otherwise, you were going to be shot if you don't respond. So everybody was running-- you know, like everybody was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say plaques, do you mean signs in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would just put up a sign and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they want you to do. And that's what you had to do. You had to follow them. Because everybody was afraid, really, that they're going to shoot you. I mean, nobody ever went through anything like this in their life before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you and your family go, or you and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family didn't have to go. It was mostly for the single people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So from there, they came over-- you know, one of the high-ranking Germans. And they would pick. They just looked at you and say, out. [INAUDIBLE] you out. You go to one side and you wait until they had how many-- whatever they needed-- 100 to 200 or 300. And they said that they're going to take us away to work. And they're going to bring us home in the-- later in the day. But that never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Glucksman, let's just stop here for a moment and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.
